title: Writing - Part 1: Media Literacy
description: Your class has been discussing the importance of media literacy and the dangers of misinformation in the digital age.

content.question: Write an essay discussing two of the dangers in your notes. You should explain which danger you consider most serious, giving reasons for your opinion.

opinions: False stories spread rapidly online and can mislead millions of people before they are corrected.
People increasingly only see content that confirms their existing views, making it harder to have balanced opinions.
Algorithms are designed to maximise engagement, often by showing sensational or divisive content.

structure.intro: Introduce the challenge of media literacy and state the two dangers you will discuss.
structure.body1: Discuss the first danger and explain how it affects individuals and society.
structure.body2: Discuss the second danger and compare its seriousness to the first.
structure.conclusion: State which danger you regard as most serious and what should be done about it.

content.modelAnswer:
In the digital age, the ability to critically evaluate information has become an essential skill. Yet media literacy education has not kept pace with the rapid growth of online content. Two of the most significant dangers in this landscape are the spread of fake news and the way social media algorithms promote biased content.

False information can spread with alarming speed online. A misleading story can reach millions of people within hours of being published, shaping public opinion on health, politics or social issues before any correction is made. During public health crises in particular, fake news has demonstrably caused harm by discouraging people from following expert advice.

Social media algorithms present a different but related problem. By prioritising content that generates strong emotional reactions, platforms tend to show users increasingly extreme material. Over time, this creates echo chambers in which people are exposed only to views that reinforce their existing beliefs, making constructive dialogue across different perspectives increasingly difficult.

In my opinion, algorithmic manipulation is the more serious danger. While fake news can in principle be countered through fact-checking, the structural incentives of social media platforms that reward sensationalism and division are far harder to address. Stronger regulation of how algorithms operate is essential to creating a healthier, more informed public discourse.
